title: Run Marketing Like a CMO
description: The ultimate capstone simulation. Pick a real business and build a complete marketing operation from the ground up — market analysis, positioning, channel strategy, paid and organic mix, CRM lifecycle, measurement framework, dashboard, quarterly plan, executive update, and budget recommendation. This is a compressed marketing degree in one project.
whyItMatters: This is what separates someone who has studied marketing from someone who can lead marketing. Every deliverable mirrors what a real CMO produces. Complete this project and you have a portfolio piece that demonstrates strategic thinking, financial literacy, cross-channel expertise, and executive communication — the four pillars that get marketing leaders hired.
prerequisites: Understanding of STP, 4Ps, SWOT, PESTEL, AARRR, and Porter's Five Forces
Comfort with spreadsheets and presentation tools

steps.title: Choose your business
steps.description: Pick a real business you can research publicly — an Australian SME, a D2C brand, a SaaS startup, or a local business you have access to. The more real the business, the more valuable the project. Write a one-page business overview: what they sell, who they sell to, current revenue range, team size, and competitive position.
steps.tips: If you can interview the founder or marketing lead, this project becomes 10x more valuable. Even a 30-minute conversation gives you insight no desk research can match.

steps.title: Market analysis
steps.description: Conduct a full situational analysis: SWOT (internal strengths and weaknesses), PESTEL (external macro forces), Porter's Five Forces (industry attractiveness), and competitive mapping (direct and indirect competitors). Identify the top 3 opportunities and top 3 threats. Calculate TAM, SAM, and SOM for the business.
steps.tips: Use SimilarWeb for competitor traffic, Google Trends for demand trends, and IBISWorld or ABS data for market sizing. Be specific with numbers — 'large market' is not analysis.

steps.title: Customer research and positioning
steps.description: Build 3 detailed buyer personas from real data (not assumptions). Apply the STP framework: segment the market, select target segments with clear rationale, and write a positioning statement for each. Create a perceptual map showing where the brand sits versus competitors on two key axes.
steps.tips: Use the format: 'For [target] who [need], [brand] is the [category] that [benefit] because [reason to believe].' Test your positioning statement — if a competitor could say the same thing, it is not sharp enough.

steps.title: Channel strategy — paid and organic mix
steps.description: Design the full channel mix across paid (Google Ads, Meta Ads, LinkedIn, TikTok), owned (website, email, social, blog), and earned (PR, partnerships, word-of-mouth). For each channel, define: objective, target audience, content format, frequency, budget allocation, and success metric. Justify the split between brand building (60%) and performance marketing (40%) using Binet and Field's research.
steps.tips: Map each channel to a funnel stage. Top of funnel: awareness channels (social, display, content). Middle: consideration channels (email nurture, retargeting, SEO). Bottom: conversion channels (search ads, landing pages, email flows).

steps.title: CRM and lifecycle strategy
steps.description: Design the complete customer lifecycle: acquisition → onboarding → engagement → retention → advocacy. Map the email automation flows: welcome sequence (5 emails), abandoned cart, post-purchase, win-back, and VIP program. Define the CRM segmentation strategy (RFM or behavioural) and specify what triggers each communication.
steps.tips: Think in terms of CLV, not just first purchase. A customer who buys once for $50 is worth less than one who buys 4 times for $30 each. Design your lifecycle to maximise repeat purchases and referrals.

steps.title: Measurement framework and KPI dashboard
steps.description: Build a complete measurement framework: define 5 north-star KPIs (e.g. CAC, LTV, ROAS, NPS, organic traffic growth), channel-level metrics for each active channel, and a reporting cadence (daily dashboards, weekly team reviews, monthly leadership reports, quarterly board updates). Create a live dashboard template in Looker Studio or Google Sheets.
steps.tips: Every KPI needs a target, a benchmark, and a reporting owner. If you cannot measure it, do not include it in the plan.

steps.title: 12-month campaign roadmap
steps.description: Build a quarter-by-quarter campaign roadmap. For each quarter, define: 1 major campaign, 2-3 always-on activities, seasonal opportunities, and a test-and-learn experiment. Include creative themes, channel activation plans, and budget phasing. Show how campaigns build on each other across the year.
steps.tips: Use a Gantt chart or timeline visual. Campaigns should not exist in isolation — each one should build on the learnings and audience growth from the previous quarter.

steps.title: Budget and financial model
steps.description: Create a detailed annual marketing budget broken down by: channel, campaign, quarter, and cost type (media, tools, freelancers, agency, content production). Build a simple ROI model: projected revenue from marketing activities, CAC by channel, projected LTV, and payback period. Include a sensitivity analysis showing outcomes at -20%, baseline, and +20% scenarios.
steps.tips: Present the budget as an investment case, not a cost request. Show: 'If we invest $X, we project $Y in revenue with a Z-month payback period.' This is how CMOs speak to CFOs.

steps.title: Risk register and learning agenda
steps.description: Identify the top 5 risks to the marketing plan (budget cuts, competitor moves, algorithm changes, team capacity, market shifts) and define a mitigation strategy for each. Create a learning agenda: what are the 3-5 biggest unknowns, and what experiments will you run in Q1 to resolve them?
steps.tips: The best plans acknowledge uncertainty. A risk register shows maturity. A learning agenda shows intellectual curiosity. Both are traits that senior leaders value highly.

steps.title: Executive presentation — CEO and CFO views
steps.description: Create two presentation decks. The CEO summary (8-10 slides): vision, strategy, key initiatives, market position, growth trajectory, and team plan. The CFO budget view (5-7 slides): investment case, ROI projections, budget breakdown, payback period, and scenario analysis. Practice delivering each in under 10 minutes.
steps.tips: The CEO cares about growth, market position, and competitive advantage. The CFO cares about returns, efficiency, and risk. Same plan, different framing. This skill — translating strategy for different stakeholders — is what makes CMOs effective.

deliverables: Market analysis with SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces, and TAM/SAM/SOM
3 detailed buyer personas with STP framework and perceptual map
Channel strategy document with paid/organic mix and budget allocation
CRM lifecycle map with 5 automated email flows designed
KPI dashboard (live in Looker Studio or Google Sheets)
Annual marketing budget with ROI model and sensitivity analysis
Learning agenda with Q1 experiment designs

rubric.description: Every tactic ties to an objective, every objective supports a business goal. The plan tells one coherent story from market insight to execution.
Analysis demonstrates genuine research — real competitor data, real market sizing, real customer insight. No generic statements.
Channel strategy shows understanding of paid vs organic, brand vs performance, and how channels work together across the funnel.
Budget is detailed, justified, and modelled with ROI projections. Sensitivity analysis shows strategic thinking under uncertainty.
The plan goes beyond acquisition to cover retention, loyalty, and advocacy. CRM flows are specific and trigger-based.
Every initiative has clear KPIs, targets, and a measurement plan. Dashboard is functional and tells a story at a glance.
CEO and CFO presentations are sharp, concise, and tailored to each audience. You can present each in under 10 minutes.
Risk register is realistic and mitigation strategies are actionable. Learning agenda shows intellectual honesty about unknowns.
